stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^3 \) be the linear transformation \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \), where \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] & [[m14]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] & [[m24]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] & [[m34]] \end{bmatrix}. \] What is the dimension of the kernel of \( T \)?
solution: The kernel of \( T \) is the null space of \( A \). Since \( A \) has \( 4 \) columns and rank \( [[rankval]] \), the kernel has dimension \( 4 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rank of \( A \) (the dimension of the image), not the kernel.
This overcounts the free variables by one.
This undercounts the free variables by one.
This is the dimension of the domain, not the kernel.
This assumes \( T \) is injective without checking.
This guesses a fixed value without applying rank–nullity.
This overcounts the free variables.
This guesses a fixed value without applying rank–nullity.

stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^4 \to \mathbb{R}^3 \) be the linear transformation \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \), where \[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] & [[m14]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] & [[m24]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] & [[m34]] \end{bmatrix}. \] What is the dimension of the image of \( T \)?
solution: The image of \( T \) is the column space of \( A \), whose dimension is \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the dimension of the KERNEL (via rank–nullity), not the image.
This overcounts the rank by one.
This undercounts the rank by one.
This is the dimension of the domain, as if \( T \) were injective onto its image.
This assumes \( T \) is the zero map.
This assumes the image fills all of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) without checking.
This overcounts the rank by two.

stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) be the linear transformation \( T(x_1, x_2, x_3) = ([[a11]]x_1 + [[a12]]x_2 + [[a13]]x_3,\ [[a21]]x_1 + [[a22]]x_2 + [[a23]]x_3) \). What is the first coordinate of \( T([[x1]], [[x2]], [[x3]]) \)?
solution: Substitute directly: the first coordinate of \( T([[x1]],[[x2]],[[x3]]) \) is \( ([[a11]])([[x1]]) + ([[a12]])([[x2]]) + ([[a13]])([[x3]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes the SECOND coordinate of \( T(\mathbf{x}) \), not the first.
This flips the sign of the middle term.
This adds the matrix entries instead of applying them to \( \mathbf{x} \).
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by two from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: Let \( T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) be the linear transformation \( T(x, y, z) = ([[a]]x + [[b]]y + [[c]]z,\ [[d]]x + [[e]]y + [[f]]z) \). For what value of \( x \) does the vector \( (x, y, [[k]]) \) lie in the kernel of \( T \)?
solution: The vector lies in the kernel exactly when both \( T \)-components vanish: \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y + [[c]]([[k]]) = 0 \) and \( [[d]]x + [[e]]y + [[f]]([[k]]) = 0 \). Solving this system gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just repeats the given third coordinate, ignoring the kernel condition.
This negates the given third coordinate without solving the system.
This multiplies coefficients without solving for \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by two from the correct value.
